These uprisings, organized into [[:en:Ikki|alliances]] to make political demands of rulers (the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] or [[shugo]] lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, [[:en:Jizamurai|Jizamurai]] (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural [[natural rights|right]], and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1428|1428]], the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of [[:en:1441|1441]], the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1457|1457]], the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1462|1462]], and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1478|1478]] and [[:en:1480|1480]].
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.

Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11422932]] Wikidata name: tsuchi-ikki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsuchi Ikki''' (土一揆, also pronounced つちいっき or どいっき) refers to the political demands of the populace that arose during the mid-to-late [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Overview ==
Around the mid-Muromachi period, societal changes brought about by the development of a market economy, advances in agricultural productivity, and the strengthening of [[:en:sō|sō]] (autonomous village unions) led to the emergence of popular uprisings in advanced areas such as the Kinai region. These uprisings, organized into [[:en:Ikki|alliances]] to make political demands of rulers (the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] or [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, [[:en:Jizamurai|Jizamurai]] (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural [[:en:natural rights|right]], and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1428|1428]], the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of [[:en:1441|1441]], the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1457|1457]], the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1462|1462]], and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1478|1478]] and [[:en:1480|1480]].
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|和書 |author = [[Satoshi Sakata (historian)|Satoshi Sakata]] |title = The History of Alliances and Unions in Japan |volume = 1 |year = 2006 |chapter = Ikki and Ichimi Dōshin |series = The World History of Alliances |publisher = Yamakawa Shuppansha |editor = [[Azio Fukuda]] |isbn = 4634444100 |ref = harv }} == See Also == * [[:en:Ikki|Ikki]] * [[Tokuseirei]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土一揆]][[zh:土一揆]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11422932]] Wikidata name: tsuchi-ikki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsuchi Ikki''' (土一揆) refers to the political demands of the populace that arose during the mid-to-late [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Overview ==
Around the mid-Muromachi period, societal changes brought about by the development of a market economy, advances in agricultural productivity, and the strengthening of [[:en:sō|sō]] (autonomous village unions) led to the emergence of popular uprisings in advanced areas such as the Kinai region. These uprisings, organized into [[:en:Ikki|alliances]] to make political demands of rulers (the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] or [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, [[:en:Jizamurai|Jizamurai]] (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural [[:en:natural rights|right]], and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1428|1428]], the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of [[:en:1441|1441]], the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1457|1457]], the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1462|1462]], and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1478|1478]] and [[:en:1480|1480]].
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|和書 |author = [[Satoshi Sakata (historian)|Satoshi Sakata]] |title = The History of Alliances and Unions in Japan |volume = 1 |year = 2006 |chapter = Ikki and Ichimi Dōshin |series = The World History of Alliances |publisher = Yamakawa Shuppansha |editor = [[Azio Fukuda]] |isbn = 4634444100 |ref = harv }} == See Also == * [[:en:Ikki|Ikki]] * [[Tokuseirei]]
[[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|土一揆|version=103263391|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Muromachi period]]
}}

These uprisings, organized into [[:en:Ikki|alliances]] to make political demands of rulers (the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] or [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, [[:en:Jizamurai|Jizamurai]] (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural [[:en:natural rights|right]], and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1428|1428]], the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of [[:en:1441|1441]], the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1457|1457]], the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1462|1462]], and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1478|1478]] and [[:en:1480|1480]].
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
== References == {{Reflist}}

== Overview ==
Around the mid-Muromachi period, societal changes brought about by the development of a market economy, advances in agricultural productivity, and the strengthening of [[:en:sō|sō]] (autonomous village unions) led to the emergence of popular uprisings in advanced areas such as the Kinai region. These uprisings, organized into [[:en:Ikki|alliances]] to make political demands of rulers (the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] or [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, [[:en:Jizamurai|Jizamurai]] (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural [[:en:natural rights|right]], and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1428|1428]], the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of [[:en:1441|1441]], the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1457|1457]], the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1462|1462]], and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1478|1478]] and [[:en:1480|1480]].
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|和書 |author = [[Satoshi Sakata (historian)|Satoshi Sakata]] |title = The History of Alliances and Unions in Japan |volume = 1 |year = 2006 |chapter = Ikki and Ichimi Dōshin |series = The World History of Alliances |publisher = Yamakawa Shuppansha |editor = [[Azio Fukuda]] |isbn = 4634444100 |ref = harv }} == See Also == * [[:en:Ikki|Ikki]] * [[Tokuseirei]]

== Overview ==
Around the mid-Muromachi period, societal changes brought about by the development of a market economy, advances in agricultural productivity, and the strengthening of [[:en:sō|sō]] (autonomous village unions) led to the emergence of popular uprisings in advanced areas such as the Kinai region. These uprisings, organized into [[:en:Ikki|alliances]] to make political demands of rulers (the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] or [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, [[:en:Jizamurai|Jizamurai]] (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural [[:en:natural rights|right]], and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1428|1428]], the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of [[:en:1441|1441]], the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1457|1457]], the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1462|1462]], and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1478|1478]] and [[:en:1480|1480]].
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|和書 |author = [[Satoshi Sakata (historian)|Satoshi Sakata]] |title = The History of Alliances and Unions in Japan |volume = 1 |year = 2006 |chapter = Ikki and Ichimi Dōshin |series = The World History of Alliances |publisher = Yamakawa Shuppansha |editor = [[Azio Fukuda]] |isbn = 4634444100 |ref = harv }} == See Also == * [[:en:Ikki|Ikki]] * [[Tokuseirei]]

Around the mid-Muromachi period, societal changes brought about by the development of a market economy, advances in agricultural productivity, and the strengthening of [[:en:sō|sō]] (autonomous village unions) led to the emergence of popular uprisings in advanced areas such as the Kinai region. These uprisings, organized into [[:en:Ikki|alliances]] to make political demands of rulers (the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] or [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, [[:en:Jizamurai|Jizamurai]] (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural [[:en:natural rights|right]], and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1428|1428]], the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of [[:en:1441|1441]], the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1457|1457]], the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1462|1462]], and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of [[:en:1478|1478]] and [[:en:1480|1480]].
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|和書 |author = [[Satoshi Sakata (historian)|Satoshi Sakata]] |title = The History of Alliances and Unions in Japan |volume = 1 |year = 2006 |chapter = Ikki and Ichimi Dōshin |series = The World History of Alliances |publisher = Yamakawa Shuppansha |editor = [[Azio Fukuda]] |isbn = 4634444100 |ref = harv }} == See Also == * [[:en:Ikki|Ikki]] * [[Tokuseirei]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土一揆]][[zh:土一揆]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土一揆]][[zh:土一揆]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土一揆]][[zh:土一揆]]
'''Tsuchi Ikki''' (土一揆) refers to the political demands of the populace that arose during the mid-to-late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}.
== Overview ==
Around the mid-Muromachi period, societal changes brought about by the development of a market economy, advances in agricultural productivity, and the strengthening of {{ill|sō|en|sō|WD=Q1717435|lt=sō}} (autonomous village unions) led to the emergence of popular uprisings in advanced areas such as the Kinai region. These uprisings, organized into {{ill|Ikki|en|Ikki|WD=Q395298|lt=alliances}} to make political demands of rulers (the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate}} or {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}} lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, {{ill|Jizamurai|en|Jizamurai|WD=Q13432011|lt=Jizamurai}} (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural {{ill|natural rights|en|natural rights|WD=Q20859012|lt=right}}, and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1428|en|1428|WD=Q6315|lt=1428}}, the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of {{ill|1441|en|1441|WD=Q6402|lt=1441}}, the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1457|en|1457|WD=Q6518|lt=1457}}, the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1462|en|1462|WD=Q6546|lt=1462}}, and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1478|en|1478|WD=Q6617|lt=1478}} and {{ill|1480|en|1480|WD=Q6628|lt=1480}}.
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|和書 |author = [[Satoshi Sakata (historian)|Satoshi Sakata]] |title = The History of Alliances and Unions in Japan |volume = 1 |year = 2006 |chapter = Ikki and Ichimi Dōshin |series = The World History of Alliances |publisher = Yamakawa Shuppansha |editor = [[Azio Fukuda]] |isbn = 4634444100 |ref = harv }} == See Also == * {{ill|Ikki|en|Ikki|WD=Q395298|lt=Ikki}} * [[Tokuseirei]]
{{translated page|ja|土一揆|version=103263391|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1428|en|1428|WD=Q6315|lt=1428|1=1428}}, the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of {{ill|1441|en|1441|WD=Q6402|lt=1441|1=1441}}, the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1457|en|1457|WD=Q6518|lt=1457|1=1457}}, the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1462|en|1462|WD=Q6546|lt=1462|1=1462}}, and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1478|en|1478|WD=Q6617|lt=1478|1=1478}} and {{ill|1480|en|1480|WD=Q6628|lt=1480|1=1480}}.
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Tsuchi Ikki''' (土一揆) refers to the political demands of the populace that arose during the mid-to-late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}.
== Overview ==
Around the mid-Muromachi period, societal changes brought about by the development of a market economy, advances in agricultural productivity, and the strengthening of {{ill|sō|en|sō|WD=Q1717435|lt=sō|1=Sō}} (autonomous village unions) led to the emergence of popular uprisings in advanced areas such as the Kinai region. These uprisings, organized into {{ill|Ikki|en|Ikki|WD=Q395298|lt=alliances|1=Ikki}} to make political demands of rulers (the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}} or {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}} lords), were called Tsuchi Ikki. During this period, as seen in the formation of [[sōson|autonomous villages]], peasants’ sense of solidarity and self-governance had increased. In this context, peasants, {{ill|Jizamurai|en|Jizamurai|WD=Q13432011|lt=Jizamurai|1=Jizamurai}} (local samurai), and [[bajime]] (horse traders) banded together in these uprisings. The "Tsuchi" in "Tsuchi Ikki" refers to the term "tsuchimin" (土民), which was used to describe farmers and peasants at the time.
Most Tsuchi Ikki revolts demanded the implementation of [[Tokusei]]. For this reason, they are also referred to as '''[[Tokusei Ikki]]'''. At the time, there was a widespread belief that ownership of movable and immovable property should revert to its original owner, even after being sold or traded. This belief formed the basis of the [[Tokusei]] ideology, which emphasized a return to the rightful state of affairs. For peasants, demanding Tokusei was seen as a natural {{ill|natural rights|en|natural rights|WD=Q20859012|lt=right|1=Natural rights}}, and economic hardships were not the primary cause of these uprisings. Many Tsuchi Ikki occurred during transitions in power, such as the ascension of a new emperor or shogun, which were seen as opportunities to implement Tokusei. These revolts became increasingly frequent, occurring almost annually.
The frequent uprisings weakened the power of the shogunate. However, the shogunate’s response was slow. Among the [[shugo daimyo]] tasked with suppression, some, sympathetic to the plight of peasants due to their prolonged residence in Kyoto, defected to the ikki side. This prompted the shogunate to repeatedly order daimyōs to exercise strict control over their subordinates. Shortly before the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, incidents such as the destruction of storehouses by soldiers in Kyoto, known as "private Tokusei," also occurred. Initially, the shogunate prohibited Tokusei and continued to collect taxes from storehouses and sake merchants. However, as uprisings increased, the shogunate eventually tolerated them and issued the "Bun’ichi Tokuseirei" (a Tokusei order allowing parties in debt disputes to pay one-tenth of the debt to the shogunate and settle the rest freely). This forced storehouses, temples, and others targeted by uprisings to hire private guards for self-defense.
Notable examples of Tsuchi Ikki include the [[Shōchō Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1428|en|1428|WD=Q6315|lt=1428|1=1428}}, the [[Kakitsu Tokusei Ikki]] of {{ill|1441|en|1441|WD=Q6402|lt=1441|1=1441}}, the [[Chōroku Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1457|en|1457|WD=Q6518|lt=1457|1=1457}}, the [[Kanshō Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1462|en|1462|WD=Q6546|lt=1462|1=1462}}, and the [[Yamashiro Tsuchi Ikki]] of {{ill|1478|en|1478|WD=Q6617|lt=1478|1=1478}} and {{ill|1480|en|1480|WD=Q6628|lt=1480|1=1480}}.
Uprisings led by [[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]] groups, which spread similarly, are referred to as [[Kokujin Ikki]].
== Evaluation of Tsuchi Ikki ==
Regarding the ideals of Tsuchi Ikki, current research predominantly evaluates these as part of a broader agricultural struggle rooted in [[sōson]] autonomy{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}.
Conversely, Yasuhiko Inagaki, in his work "Japanese Medieval Social History Theory," argued that legal disputes and dispersal conflicts at the individual manor level constituted the primary form of medieval peasant struggles, asserting that large-scale Tokusei Ikki were not peasant revolts. Unlike the strong organizational structure created through rituals like [[Ichimi Shinsui]] seen in Kokujin Ikki and peasant Ikki, Tsuchi Ikki lacked a unified leadership structure due to the diverse social strata involved. While individual peasants participated, collective village involvement in Tsuchi Ikki was less significant than commonly perceived{{Sfn|Sakata|2006|pp=2-42}}, and external requests for participation were carefully judged based on prevailing circumstances.
